<!-- Topic 3: Binary Records -->
<!-- Slides 72–106 -->

# Binary Records
<!-- Slide 72 -->

## Can We Read the 500th Record Directly? {.smaller}

+ A text file normally requires reading earlier records first.
+ Can a fixed-size record turn a file into random-access storage?

::: notes
Slides 72–106
:::

<!-- Slide 73 -->

---

## A File as an Array of Records

| Record 0 | Record 1 | Record 2 | Record 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| same bytes | same bytes | same bytes | same bytes |

+ Equal-sized slots make a record’s starting position calculable.

<!-- Slide 74 -->

---

## Fixed Size Is the Key Invariant

```cpp
byteOffset = recordIndex * sizeof(Record);
```

+ Every stored record must occupy exactly `sizeof(Record)` bytes.
+ Record indexes begin at zero.

<!-- Slide 75 -->

---

## Designing a Disk Record

```cpp
struct EmployeeRecord {
    int employeeID;
    char name[40];
    double hourlyRate;
    bool active;
};
```

+ Fixed-size members keep every record the same size.

<!-- Slide 76 -->

---

## Why Not Store `string` Directly?

```cpp
struct UnsafeRecord {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
};
```

+ A `string` object contains management state; its characters may live elsewhere.
+ Raw bytes do not reconstruct that external storage.

<!-- Slide 77 -->

---

## Memory Record Versus Disk Record

```cpp
struct Employee {
    int employeeID;
    string name;
    double hourlyRate;
};

struct EmployeeRecord {
    int employeeID;
    char name[40];
    double hourlyRate;
    bool active;
};
```

+ The convenient program representation need not match the storage representation.

<!-- Slide 78 -->

---

## Opening a Binary File

```cpp
ofstream output("employees.dat", ios::binary);
ifstream input("employees.dat", ios::binary);

fstream file("employees.dat",
             ios::in | ios::out | ios::binary);
```

<!-- Slide 79 -->

---

## Writing One Record

```cpp
EmployeeRecord worker {104, "Ana Ruiz", 27.50, true};

output.write(
    reinterpret_cast<const char*>(&worker),
    sizeof(worker)
);
```

<!-- Slide 80 -->

---

## Writing an Array of Records

```cpp
for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    output.write(
        reinterpret_cast<const char*>(&staff[i]),
        sizeof(staff[i])
    );
}
```

<!-- Slide 81 -->

---

## Reading One Record

```cpp
EmployeeRecord worker {};

input.read(
    reinterpret_cast<char*>(&worker),
    sizeof(worker)
);

if (input)
    cout << worker.name << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 82 -->

---

## Reading Until the File Ends

```cpp
EmployeeRecord worker {};

while (input.read(
           reinterpret_cast<char*>(&worker),
           sizeof(worker))) {
    cout << worker.employeeID << "  "
         << worker.name << '\n';
}
```

<!-- Slide 83 -->

---

## Reading an Entire Record File

```cpp
int count = 0;

while (count < CAPACITY &&
       input.read(
           reinterpret_cast<char*>(&staff[count]),
           sizeof(staff[count]))) {
    count++;
}
```

<!-- Slide 84 -->

---

## The Record Offset Formula

```cpp
streamoff offset =
    static_cast<streamoff>(recordIndex) *
    sizeof(EmployeeRecord);
```

+ Human record 1 corresponds to index 0.

<!-- Slide 85 -->

---

## Seeking to a Record

```cpp
input.seekg(offset, ios::beg);
```

+ `seekg` moves the get position used for reading.
+ `ios::beg` makes the offset relative to the file’s beginning.

<!-- Slide 86 -->

---

## Reading One Selected Record

```cpp
input.seekg(offset, ios::beg);
input.read(
    reinterpret_cast<char*>(&worker),
    sizeof(worker)
);

if (input)
    cout << worker.name << '\n';
```

<!-- Slide 87 -->

---

## How Many Records Are in the File?

```cpp
input.seekg(0, ios::end);
streamoff fileSize = input.tellg();
int recordCount = -1;

if (fileSize >= 0 &&
    fileSize % sizeof(EmployeeRecord) == 0) {
    recordCount = static_cast<int>(
        fileSize / sizeof(EmployeeRecord));
}
input.seekg(0, ios::beg);
```

+ `-1` reports an invalid or incomplete record file.

<!-- Slide 88 -->

---

## Valid Record Boundaries

```cpp
if (recordIndex < 0 || recordIndex >= recordCount) {
    cout << "Invalid record number\n";
} else {
    // Seek and read.
}
```

+ Valid indexes are `0` through `recordCount - 1`.

<!-- Slide 89 -->

---

## Updating a Record in Place

```cpp
worker.hourlyRate = 30.00;

file.seekp(offset, ios::beg);
file.write(
    reinterpret_cast<const char*>(&worker),
    sizeof(worker)
);
```

<!-- Slide 90 -->

---

## Input and Output Cursors

| Operation | Cursor |
|---|---|
| `read`, `seekg`, `tellg` | Get position |
| `write`, `seekp`, `tellp` | Put position |

+ Clear failed stream state before attempting another seek.

<!-- Slide 91 -->

---

## Organizing a Record Program

```cpp
int recordCount(ifstream& input);
bool readRecord(ifstream& input, int index,
                EmployeeRecord& worker);
bool updateRecord(fstream& file, int index,
                  const EmployeeRecord& worker);
void printRecord(const EmployeeRecord& worker);
```

+ Each function owns one file-positioning responsibility.

<!-- Slide 92 -->

---

## Padding Becomes Part of the File

```cpp
cout << sizeof(EmployeeRecord) << '\n';
```

+ Raw binary output includes compiler-inserted padding.
+ Changing member order can change the bytes and record size.

<!-- Slide 93 -->

---

## The Same Program Must Read the File

+ The reader must agree on member types, sizes, order, and padding.
+ Compiler, platform, architecture, and byte order can change those details.
+ Raw records are safest within one controlled program environment.

<!-- Slide 94 -->

---

## Pointers Must Never Be Serialized This Way

```cpp
struct BadRecord {
    int employeeID;
    char* name;
};
```

+ The stored address is meaningless in a later process.

<!-- Slide 95 -->

---

## Raw Binary Versus Defined File Formats

| Raw object bytes | Defined field format |
|---|---|
| Quick in one environment | Portable by design |
| Layout dependent | Layout explicitly specified |
| Difficult to evolve | Can include versions |

<!-- Slide 96 -->

---

## Safety Before Raw Serialization

+ Confirm that the record is trivially copyable.
+ Define fixed-size text fields and failure behavior.
+ Plan for partial records, deletion, and format changes.

<!-- Slide 97 -->

---

## Binary Does Not Mean Secret

+ Binary mode reads and writes bytes without text translation.
+ It does not encrypt the data.
+ It does not guarantee compression.
+ Anyone who knows the layout can interpret the records.

<!-- Slide 98 -->

---

## Only Trivially Copyable Records

```cpp
#include <type_traits>

static_assert(
    is_trivially_copyable<EmployeeRecord>::value,
    "EmployeeRecord cannot be written as raw bytes"
);
```

<!-- Slide 99 -->

---

## Safe Fixed-Size Text Fields

```cpp
#include <cstring>

strncpy(record.name, sourceName.c_str(),
        sizeof(record.name) - 1);
record.name[sizeof(record.name) - 1] = '\0';
string restoredName = record.name;
```

+ Reserve one byte for the null terminator.
+ Overlong source names are intentionally truncated.

<!-- Slide 100 -->

---

## Appending One Record

```cpp
ofstream output("employees.dat",
                ios::binary | ios::app);

output.write(
    reinterpret_cast<const char*>(&worker),
    sizeof(worker)
);
```

<!-- Slide 101 -->

---

## Short and Corrupted Records

```cpp
input.read(reinterpret_cast<char*>(&worker),
           sizeof(worker));

if (input.gcount() != sizeof(worker)) {
    cout << "Incomplete record\n";
}
```

<!-- Slide 102 -->

---

## Deleting a Record Is Different

+ Rewrite the remaining records into a replacement file; or
+ set an `active` member to `false` and reuse the slot later.
+ A tombstone preserves fixed offsets but leaves unused slots.

<!-- Slide 103 -->

---

## Changing the Structure Changes the Format

+ Adding, removing, reordering, or resizing a member changes the record layout.
+ Store a format version when files must outlive one program build.
+ Convert old records rather than interpreting them with a new layout.

<!-- Slide 104 -->

---

## When Should We Use Binary Records?

+ Use them for fixed-layout local data under controlled conditions.
+ Use an explicitly defined format when files must survive program changes or move between systems.
+ Never assume raw serialization is universally portable.

<!-- Slide 105 -->

---

## Binary Records Summary

+ Equal-sized records make byte offsets and direct access possible.
+ Raw serialization requires a fixed, trivially copyable layout.
+ Portability, validation, deletion, and format evolution remain explicit design responsibilities.

<!-- Slide 106 -->
